title: BBC Says Sorry for Bad Word on TV
summary: The BBC, a TV company, said they made a mistake during a Bafta awards show. A person with Tourette's shouted a very mean racial word while actors were giving out an award. This bad word was not taken out of the TV broadcast that aired later on BBC One and stayed on iPlayer, a streaming service, until the next morning. Many people complained about this. The BBC's complaints unit said this was wrong and broke their rules, but it was not done on purpose. The team editing the show did not hear the word, so they did not remove it. They did remove another use of the same word later. The BBC said leaving the show on iPlayer for so long made the problem worse. They promised to learn from this and make their processes better to stop it from happening again.

an awards show for movies and TV
a very mean word about someone's race
a service to watch BBC shows online
a group that listens to people's problems

What made the problem worse according to the BBC?
options: The show stayed on iPlayer overnight
questions.correct_answer: The show stayed on iPlayer overnight

questions.question: What will the BBC do after this mistake?

background_read: The BBC is a big TV and radio company in the UK that makes shows and news. The Bafta awards are a special event where movies and TV shows get prizes. Sometimes, people say mean words by accident, like in this case with Tourette's, which can make others feel hurt. TV companies have rules to protect viewers from offensive content.
Article_Structure: WHO: The BBC and a person with Tourette's
WHAT: A racial slur was broadcast on TV and iPlayer
WHERE: On BBC One and iPlayer during the Bafta awards
WHY: It was a mistake because the editing team did not hear the word

perspectives.description: The BBC admits it was a serious mistake but says it was not intentional and they will improve.
